speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. You were telling me about when you first became aware of the war, and how it affected you, what you life-- how your life changed. And I want to take you back a little bit more and ask you, did you know that there was a war, or that there were any differences in your life in Kostopol before your father asked all of you to come together and-- and informed you there was a war? Did you notice anything different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- what do you mean, is it different? When-- before, when my father-- sure, it was a big different. Food, we didn't have already. And every little-- every little-- once in a day, my mother gave us sliced bread. And she used to give [NON-ENGLISH] bread for us smaller kids. And we used to go to the Ukrainian people to-- not to ask them to give me or to trade for-- for this stuff, what we had, silver, candle-- light the candles. And my mother took all the best stuff. You know, when you run away, they used to take the gold and every--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't-- they-- some gave us a bread for-- for everything. You know? And how long can you survive, a bread for seven people? If you-- And if somebody was besides you, you cannot leave them. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: We understood what is war. I mean, you may have to fight for our-- my father told us, you have to fight for living. If somebody tell you not to go in here, to hide, go in. Force yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still living at home at that time in Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- when we left the house, we left-- we thought maybe we would come back. But we never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit more about before you left the house. What it was like living, knowing that there was a war going on. How did it affect you as a little girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not a baby no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That mean you didn't feel a baby. You needed to-- to act like a grown-up person. If it's not food, there's no food then you know, you're not eating. Then you knew it's war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: More hungry. More-- you're wearing the same clothes for one month. Didn't have where to change. You didn't have the clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still play with the [INAUDIBLE] little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: With what?

speaker: interviewer
text: The little boy next door. Did you go and play with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go sometimes, sometimes not. More sad. You didn't see the happiness on the face. You recognize a child, from four or five years old, was a grownup person already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to anybody about how you were feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept everything to yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of changes did you see in your parents and your sisters and your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: In every part, like, in-- in-- big change, like. Everybody was going to home, to-- to the neighbors, with talking, what to do, how to survive, what to do with the children. Everybody want to help the children first. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a very big-- Ukrainian, lots of Ukrainian, went in, in Jewish homes. And they took off everything, from the houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that happening in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my grandfather's home I saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- not in our.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do in your grandfather's home?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took everything. They took the-- what they like there, the lamps. They took the tables. You don't need it. You-- you're going to die, they said. And I came running. And I told my mother, he said me going to die. All people, better to keep them off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said, he's-- he's older than you. He can die before you. You don't know what it mean, to die. He's not God. God tells us-- you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In our town, the Ukrainian people did more like what the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukrainian people killed more, the people. Which in our town was very little German. Very-- not  too many soldier, German. And the Ukrainian, that's what they did. They picked up all the people, what left. They took them on that playground. They told the men to take-- that time maybe two German soldier that was. All Ukrainian. The women, they threw them in. And they buried them. The earth was cracking. And then they told the man to-- to dig a hole. And they shot the men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this with your own eyes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it. My brother saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were running-- we were already not-- that's what I told you. My parents left my brother to look after your father and mother. And we thought we wouldn't come back. But that's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you leave? What happened that day, that your parents decided you should all leave except for your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That day was the day my father said if we're not going to leave, they're going to kill us. If not the German, the Ukrainian. They made their self like, a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know? And they knew the Jewish people at their house have silver and gold. And that's what they wanted. And-- Do you remember how you felt that day, that he said you were going to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't understand too much. We were told we were going to leave. And we're going to come back. I really felt war when we left and we start not to have food. And we need it to-- give it from-- my mother took off the ring to-- to give it for a piece of bread. And the Ukrainian didn't give us, even, if they saw us to come besides their door, they closed. And they closed the-- the windows not to-- like, they didn't want to talk to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people who helped you or were friends of yours before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was another city. And one Polish lady, she was not Polish. She was Ukrainian. She helped me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your family at this point in time, when she helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went with your parents and your brothers, and your sister. And you left your home in Kostopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Tyczyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we walked to Tyczyn. And from Tyczyn, [? Zaleszany. ?] And we went to-- to Kiev.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was that? How many-- did it take--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I think it's 350 kilometer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you walked?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. Every day we slept in all kind of places, outside. And we came there. And that's what they told us. And we would come to Kiev. There they're going-- give us-- Russia, going to send us to homes. And we didn't have a choice. And with us was my mother's sister, Ruchel. And she had a daughter. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ruchel's daughter, do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dobcza. And she was young-- very young. She was younger from me. She was two years old, even a year and  a half. And we took them with us. And we were there grand-- underground. And she cried a lot. She wanted food. We were older. And we understood we can't have food. But she cried. She wanted food. And the people didn't want it, to be with us. They said she should leave. We should-- thanks to her, they're going to find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So underground, you were with other families too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, three families. And somebody wanted to kill the little girl with a cushion, to cover her. And she woke up. And she saw that. She couldn't. She said, I'm not killing my child. And she took her daughter. And she left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Germans-- the Germans killed her, on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening, that they were going to try and kill your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I was sleeping. I would-- that's what my mother told us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's not a good feeling. I want to tell you not once I wished somebody would kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your family was still together? You were still with your mother and your father a that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were still-- yeah. That time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we-- we left that place. And we-- we tried to go around the mountains. And we went to Kiev. And that's-- they said the train is only for woman-- older woman and for younger kids. And my father-- my mother tried to dress my mother-- my father like a woman, put a babushka, and to put here a dress. And we would try to go. And that time, the German will start firing on the train. Lots got killed on the train who went in. And who didn't go was lucky. And we would start-- that time, we ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- so did anybody from your family go on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were running, that time, anyplace. And from that time, I never saw my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ran on your own, by yourself. How did you know where to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to-- to a family. And the family said, we try to go in the mountains here. back-- we didn't go far. And we went back to the city, to closest there, where to hide.

speaker: interviewer
text: The city, Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in Kiev. But we didn't go farther. We went back to our city, like. And there is more places, where to-- to hide. And the family took me with them. I went with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The family was Barul, Yenta.

speaker: interviewer
text: A Jewish family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a Jewish family. And I got lost there, from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were running. The German find the place. And somebody came and said, we have to run. The German knows we are here. And we didn't know so many people were there. And when we were running, I saw so many people, maybe 100 people was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody thought that they are alone here, hiding. You know? Somebody came and told we have to run away-- away from here. Any place, and one man stand up and said, the best place to go in, in the water. And how could you breathe? You can't stay too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told us, you have to cut off one straw, that, what is growing in the water. And you hold the straw in your mouth. And you can be under the water. And you can breathe. And that's the way I was 2-and-a-1/2 hours in the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: What season was it? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What season? What time of year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was cold already. It was not snow. But it was cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you thinking, when you were underwater?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't thinks a person think, that time. You're just like an animal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You-- you're jealous. You see an animal, they are more protected than you. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I went up, I didn't see nobody. Then I was alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were under the water with other people. But when you came up--

speaker: interviewee
text: The people went out alone. They didn't kept-- nobody-- you know, only-- the only-- I think the only person who care is a mother and a father. But not stranger. People would just think for their family. They not going to think I saw another little girl. I didn't thinks they remember, even, I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you felt entirely alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of day did you come up?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the sun. I don't know. Like, it was still the sun. And I was shaking. I didn't have my-- clothes. And I lost one shoe in-- in the-- I was deep. There was mud. And one, you know-- and I had black shoe, what it's closed. And I lost one shoe inside. And I went out with one shoe. I tried to find it, to look after the shoe, and I couldn't. I-- I took-- I took from me, the underwear. And I cover my foot. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was-- it was not a good feeling. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next? Did you join up with any people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was for six-- for four month, I was with that Polish lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, for a little while, I find my father, in the underground. My father went with me to look for food. And Polish people-- I don't know if it was German or was Polish. They screamed out, out! My father threw me in-- in a-- in a garden with corn. And-- and I heard my father said, what you're going to hear, don't stand up. And that was full with water, you know, from rain. And I heard shooting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's all. And it was scare. I think I passed out. And I was there. And in the morning, the people came, taking corn for the cow, to feed them. And the lady find me. She took me in. She told everybody there I'm your sister's little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took a false identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She had a daughter and she had a son. The son was very good. Only the daughter was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of this family and these people? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- the kibbutz send them-- used to send them-- I had the-- the name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They used to send them, every Christmas, the oranges, the kibbutz. Oh, and the father died. And the mother died. I-- I don't know more. I don't have a good feeling with their daughter. She's writing letters to my sister. We're not-- I don't want to even answer that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do to make you feel so uncomfortable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the mother used to-- they used to-- they didn't go to school at that time. It was wartime, you know? And the mother used to give the boy-- everybody had a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even to me, you know? If I went cleaning where the cow was, she needed to wash the floors. They were the wooden floors. And with brushes and sand, they used to wash the floor, to make yellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what mother used to do to me. Edna, you would do this. She would do that. When the mother used to go away, she called me. And she said, you do that for me too. And I said, I'm not going to do it. She said, if you're not going do it, I'm going to tell you-- I am a Jew. And they will take you away. And they kill you. In  the night time, I used to sleep in the kitchen with-- on straw, a little bit, straw and a blanket. She used to come and take away the blanket from me. And she said, if you tell my mom I took away, you see what I'm going to do. I'm going to tell-- there was a guy, what used to go and look in the houses. I-- I'm going to tell him you are a Jew. Even with food. If the mother gave me two potatoes, and you know, with food-- she used to take the potato from me, away, and to give the-- the pig, only I should have less. She was not human. She was a bloodsucker. And the boy was very good. He was very kind to me. He used to tell me don't-- don't take serious, Maria. I was six months there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you during that period?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven, eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was the boy? And how old was Maria?

speaker: interviewee
text: The boy, he-- she was older from me. She was 11 years old. He-- he was 14 or 15. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The father was not too much in the house. He was always working. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When she get-- took me and told me I have to leave, she scared for-- she gave me a bread. And she gave me cheese. And she gave me clothes. And she gave me a blanket. And the father took me, covered me under that-- that tractor. And she took me in a-- in a place where I can hide myself. And he said, I going find a lots of Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- That was in Tyczyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did the area look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was lots of trees. They had lots of trees. All around, they had water. And the trees, lots. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were hide-- hiding there. And I was-- I find a gentlemen with-- an older man. And he had two kids. And they were not his kids, even. And he took me in, in a place where we was 12 people, underground, under a tree. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went out. We used to cover that with the grass. You know, we took out a piece of grass. And under the tree, we made a big hole. And you couldn't turn yourself. You couldn't lay down. You-- that was fro two people, maybe, a place, where there was 12 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't-- one person, and I saw him in Israel. And he passed away already. When I was-- when I was in Israel, the convention from these Kostopol people, that's where I met him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he recognized me. He said, you Chana's daughter? yeah. And you were with me. I didn't remember. Was the group that-- that stayed underground under the tree together for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- we were there, I think, for six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six weeks underneath the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the nighttime-- all day, nothing. It was just sitting and talking. People, all the people, sitting and talking. And telling us there's going to be a day we're going to sit at a table. And we're going to have bread and butter with milk. And they used to tell, Edna, you-- to you, I'm going to give you two glasses  of milk. And you're going to take out warm bread from the oven. And with that way, maybe fall asleep. [LAUGHS] We did-- we didn't talk nothing else, only about food. In the nighttime, they used to go out. Every time, somebody else. We used to go in the garbage, where the people living, to take out the-- from the potatoes, carrots, anything what was already-- people couldn't-- even a dog would not eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a turn going out at night too, at seven years old, eight years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to go then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about what that was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that was my first night, what when we went out with-- with one lady. The lady was Chaya. You know, named Chaya. And we walked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's-- we walked out to go. And we hear lots of noise. Like, heavy cars, heavy tractors going. And she said, you know what, Edna? We may have to hide, to see what is going. And it was there, a-- like, it's not like a river, but like under, you know, water from the rain. Water was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We hide there. We went in half water. And we-- she understood it's Russian. And she said, the Russian are coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, how could you know it's Russian? She said, here is a red-- on the-- on the truck-- tanks, what they had was Russian writing. And red flag, what they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went out. She said, you stay. I'm going out. And she started talking to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said to them, please, could you give us something? And they gave her. And then she saw-- only for her. And she asked me to come up. And she gave-- he gave us sugar. And he gave us bread. When he saw a child, they gave more food. And I can't-- one soldier gave a pair of boots. You know? Were they for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were big boot-- very, from soldiers, you know? Well, the-- the lady took them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel seeing soldiers, getting food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I felt like they are angels. You know, I didn't feel like they were going to kill us. They gave us food. And then they told us the Russian going-- I heard  it, what they talking, the Russian going, war with the German, to fight. And the Russia had lots of Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you go back underneath the tree, underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went back to bring the food for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you together again with this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Another week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the group organized in any kind of way?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the men used to-- the younger men, they used to go out to find out the news from other people. They used to come and tell us the news. Say how far, how far is the German-- the German-- if they are-- they told in this city, in our city is no-- no one Jewish person left. Then I knew I don't have no family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had one reunion with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you never saw him again? And you stayed with this group of people for another week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, another week. And we-- we were walking. And we knew, already, on that side is the Russian going. We went after the Russian soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they gave us food. That time, it was not bad. They gave us food. And they gave us warm water. But they told us, you can't go farther. There is the front. There we're fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did your group have any arms or ammunition? Any weapons?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. They had, only-- the men had knives. We didn't have nothing. Only a week later, [? I followed ?] [? in-- ?] in another place. There they had guns already. Just they used to kill German, and used take away the ammunition. Well, Ukrainian, they needed to fight. They didn't know from where to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a moment--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- they called partisanar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they have a specific name for their partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a name. I cannot remember the name. They had-- they had their name. And they had their-- well, it's hard to tell, to recognize people who belonged to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a moment, I'm going to ask you to tell me more about that second group that you belonged to.
